section.title: Solomon's Prosperity
section.sectionRationale: Chapters 1 to 3 have narrated Solomon's accession to David's throne and his prayer at Gibeon for a discerning heart, a prayer the LORD answered with wisdom and understanding beyond any king before or after him. Chapter 4 now shows that gift bearing fruit in the ordinary business of governing: a settled cabinet of officials, twelve district governors who provision the royal table in rotation, and a kingdom so secure and abundant that Judah and Israel eat, drink, and rejoice, each family under its own vine and fig tree. The chapter closes by returning to the source of it all, the God-given wisdom that made Solomon wiser than the sages of the East and drew kings from every nation to hear him.
section.thesis: Because the LORD grants Solomon the wise and understanding heart he asked for, the kingdom becomes ordered, secure, and abundant, so that Israel briefly enjoys the peace and plenty promised long before to Abraham, a foretaste of the true Son of David whose wisdom and reign will have no end.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the officials of Solomon's court, verses 1 to 6; the twelve district governors who supply the kingdom, verses 7 to 19; the peace, abundance, and daily provision of the realm, verses 20 to 28; and the God-given wisdom that crowns and explains it all, verses 29 to 34.

verses.text: So King Solomon ruled over Israel,
and these were his chief officials: Azariah son of Zadok was the priest;
Elihoreph and Ahijah, the sons of Shisha, were secretaries; Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was the recorder;
Benaiah son of Jehoiada was in charge of the army; Zadok and Abiathar were priests;
Azariah son of Nathan was in charge of the governors; Zabud son of Nathan was a priest and adviser to the king;
Ahishar was in charge of the palace; and Adoniram son of Abda was in charge of the forced labor.
Solomon had twelve governors over all Israel to provide food for the king and his household. Each one would arrange provisions for one month of the year,
and these were their names: Ben-hur in the hill country of Ephraim;
Ben-deker in Makaz, in Shaalbim, in Beth-shemesh, and in Elon-beth-hanan;
Ben-hesed in Arubboth (Socoh and all the land of Hepher belonged to him);
Ben-abinadab in Naphath-dor (Taphath, a daughter of Solomon, was his wife);
Baana son of Ahilud in Taanach, in Megiddo, and in all of Beth-shean next to Zarethan below Jezreel, from Beth-shean to Abel-meholah and on past Jokmeam;
Ben-geber in Ramoth-gilead (the villages of Jair son of Manasseh in Gilead belonged to him, as well as the region of Argob in Bashan with its sixty great cities with walls and bronze bars);
Ahinadab son of Iddo in Mahanaim;
Ahimaaz in Naphtali (he had married Basemath, a daughter of Solomon);
Baana son of Hushai in Asher and in Aloth;
Jehoshaphat son of Paruah in Issachar;
Shimei son of Ela in Benjamin;
Geber son of Uri in the land of Gilead, including the territories of Sihon king of the Amorites and of Og king of Bashan. There was also one governor in the land of Judah.

units.label: Peace and abundance in the land

verses.text: The people of Judah and Israel were as numerous as the sand on the seashore, and they were eating and drinking and rejoicing.
And Solomon reigned over all the kingdoms from the Euphrates to the land of the Philistines, as far as the border of Egypt. These kingdoms offered tribute and served Solomon all the days of his life.
Solomon's provisions for a single day were thirty cors of fine flour, sixty cors of meal,
ten fat oxen, twenty range oxen, and a hundred sheep, as well as deer, gazelles, roebucks, and fattened poultry.
For Solomon had dominion over everything west of the Euphrates, over all the kingdoms from Tiphsah to Gaza, and he had {{shalom|peace}} on all sides.
Throughout the days of Solomon, Judah and Israel dwelt securely from Dan to Beersheba, each man under his own vine and his own fig tree.
Solomon had 4,000 stalls for his chariot horses and 12,000 horses.
Each month the governors in turn provided food for King Solomon and all who came to his table. They saw to it that nothing was lacking.
Each one also brought to the required place their quotas of barley and straw for the chariot horses and other horses.

units.label: Solomon's God-given wisdom
units.note: wiser than all the sages of the East

verses.text: And God gave Solomon {{chokmah|wisdom}}, exceedingly deep insight, and {{tebunah|understanding}} beyond measure, like the sand on the seashore.
Solomon's wisdom was greater than that of all the men of the East, greater than all the wisdom of Egypt.
He was wiser than all men, wiser than Ethan the Ezrahite, and wiser than Heman, Calcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol. And his fame spread throughout the surrounding nations.
Solomon composed three thousand proverbs, and his songs numbered a thousand and five.
He spoke of trees, from the cedar in Lebanon to the hyssop growing in the wall, and he taught about animals, birds, reptiles, and fish.
So men of all nations came to listen to Solomon's wisdom, sent by all the kings of the earth, who had heard of his wisdom.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, the skill of living rightly and well under God, not mere cleverness. This is the very gift Solomon requested at Gibeon in chapter 3, and chapter 4 shows the LORD granting it beyond measure, so that Solomon's chokmah orders his court, keeps his kingdom secure, and draws the nations to hear him.

tebunah.gloss: Understanding or discernment, closely paired with chokmah as the practical grasp of how things truly are. The text piles up near synonyms for insight, wisdom, understanding, breadth of mind, to stress that this gift exceeds every human measure, like the sand on the seashore.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, and well-being, not merely the absence of conflict but the flourishing of a life and a land rightly ordered under God. Solomon has shalom on every side, the very rest his father David was denied, and it is this peace that finally makes temple building possible in the chapters that follow.

basic.context: Solomon has just been given wisdom by God at Gibeon, in answer to his prayer for a discerning heart. Chapter 4 shows what that wisdom produces in practice: a well-staffed court of officials, twelve district governors who take turns supplying the royal household with food, and a kingdom so peaceful and prosperous that ordinary people eat, drink, and rejoice under their own vine and fig tree. The chapter ends by circling back to the source of it all, God's own gift of wisdom, which made Solomon wiser than the wise men of the East and drew visitors from every nation who wanted to hear him speak. The key questions the chapter raises are what it means for a kingdom to flourish under a wise and God-given rule, how this list of names and numbers is meant to be read as Scripture, and how this golden but temporary peace points forward to something more lasting.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, presented largely as an administrative record: names of officials, districts, and daily provisions. It is not a manual for statecraft, but it is a deliberate theological portrait, showing in concrete, countable terms what it looks like when God's promises of a land, offspring as many as the sand of the sea, and rest from enemies come to a real, if partial and temporary, fulfillment under a wise king. Readers should resist mining the list of names for hidden meanings and instead read the chapter for its overall shape: order, security, abundance, and wisdom, all traced back to the LORD's gift.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is saturated with the vocabulary of chokmah, wisdom, and tebunah, understanding, culminating in verse 29's description of a gift like the sand on the seashore, echoing the promise to Abraham of offspring beyond counting. Verse 24 says Solomon had shalom, peace, on every side, the settled wholeness that finally lets Israel enjoy what God had long promised.
basic.typology: Solomon's wise rule that brings security, abundance, and international acclaim anticipates the greater Son of David, whose wisdom and peace will have no end and whose kingdom will draw not merely kings but every nation to worship. Solomon's wisdom was real and God-given, yet it proved temporary and was not enough to keep his own heart faithful; it points beyond itself to the wisdom of God embodied in Christ, in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.
basic.christ: Solomon is given a wisdom exceeding all the sages of his day, and kings travel from afar simply to hear him speak, verse 34. Jesus later says that one greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42, and Paul teaches that in Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, Colossians 2:3. Where Solomon's peace and plenty lasted only for a season and could not outlast his own heart's drift, Christ's wisdom is perfect and His kingdom of peace will never end, Isaiah 9:6-7.
basic.god: God is the one who gives wisdom generously, exactly as He promised Solomon, and He is faithful to the covenant promises of land, offspring, and rest that Israel now, however briefly, enjoys. He orders history not only through dramatic acts but through the quiet, unglamorous business of good administration, provision, and peace, showing that His care extends to the ordinary rhythms of daily bread as much as to great deliverances.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 15 and 22 promise Abraham offspring as numerous as the sand of the seashore, a promise this chapter echoes almost verse for verse. Deuteronomy 17 warns a future king not to multiply horses or wives, a warning this very chapter's numbers should make the reader watch carefully in the chapters ahead. Matthew 12 has Jesus declare that one greater than Solomon is here, and 1 Corinthians 1 and Colossians 2 locate true wisdom in Christ.
basic.oneStory: This chapter is a high point in Israel's story, the LORD's ancient promises to Abraham and Moses of land, numerous offspring, and rest from enemies visibly, if temporarily, coming true under a wise king. It is one more link in the long chain that carries God's purposes from Abraham through David toward the true King, Jesus Christ, in whom perfect wisdom and unending peace are finally and permanently given.

context.literary: The chapter is arranged as a careful triptych: officials, verses 1 to 6; regional governors, verses 7 to 19; and a summary of prosperity, verses 20 to 28, before a closing celebration of wisdom, verses 29 to 34, that answers back to Solomon's prayer in chapter 3. The repeated numbers and lists function rhetorically, piling up evidence of abundance and order rather than simply recording bureaucratic trivia.
context.historical: A twelve-month provisioning system, one district supplying the court for one month each year, reflects genuine administrative practice for supporting a royal household without any single region bearing the whole burden year-round. The territories named span both sides of the Jordan and reach into former Canaanite and Transjordanian lands, showing the extent of Solomon's control. The daily provisions and horse numbers in verses 22 to 26 signal immense royal wealth by ancient Near Eastern standards, comparable to other great kingdoms of the era.
context.canonical: This chapter is the high water mark of the united monarchy's prosperity, deliberately framed to echo the patriarchal promises of offspring like the sand of the sea and a land at rest, Genesis 15:5, 22:17. It also quietly plants details, many horses, many wives implied by daughters married to governors, that anticipate the very warnings of Deuteronomy 17:16-17 that Solomon will later violate, chapter 11, showing Scripture's honesty even about its brightest moments.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To display, in the concrete terms of officials, districts, provisions, and international fame, that the wisdom the LORD gave Solomon in chapter 3 truly produced an ordered, secure, and abundant kingdom, fulfilling long-standing covenant promises, while quietly setting the stage for both the temple to come and the decline still to be told.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a particular administrative arrangement and a particular season of blessing; it does not prescribe a model of monarchy or bureaucracy for every era. What endures as timeless truth is that true wisdom is God's gift, that peace and abundance flow from His covenant faithfulness, and that even great blessing can carry within it the seeds of future compromise if not held with a watchful heart.

debates.question: Should the wealth and horse numbers of verses 21 to 28 be read as unqualified praise of Solomon?

views.label: A straightforward celebration of covenant blessing
views.case: The chapter reads naturally as good news: the promises of Genesis are being fulfilled, Israel is secure and prosperous, and this is the LORD rewarding Solomon's request for wisdom rather than riches, exactly as promised in 1 Kings 3:13.

views.label: A celebration shadowed by quiet warning
views.case: The same chapter that praises Solomon's wisdom lists thousands of horses and daughters given in marriage to officials, details that brush uncomfortably close to Deuteronomy 17:16-17's warnings against a king multiplying horses and wives; the narrator may be letting the numbers speak a caution the characters themselves do not yet hear.

debates.bearing: Either way the chapter is read as real, covenantal blessing; the second view simply notes that Kings, true to its style elsewhere, often lets bare facts carry a moral weight the narrator does not spell out, preparing readers for chapter 11 without undercutting the genuine goodness of this moment.

study.originalLanguages: Twice in close succession the text piles up words for the same gift: chokmah, wisdom, and tebunah, understanding, verse 29, as if straining ordinary vocabulary to describe what God has given Solomon, a gift explicitly compared to the sand on the seashore, deliberately recalling the promise to Abraham of offspring beyond counting, Genesis 22:17. Then in verse 24 the chapter names the fruit of that wisdom as shalom, peace, on every side, the settled wholeness of a kingdom finally at rest, the very condition David longed for but never fully possessed.

typology.type: Solomon's God-given wisdom that draws the nations to hear him
typology.fulfillment: Christ, in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, whom Jesus Himself says is greater than Solomon, Matthew 12:42, Colossians 2:3.

typology.type: A kingdom at peace on every side under a wise king
typology.fulfillment: The kingdom of the Prince of Peace, whose government and peace will increase without end, Isaiah 9:6-7.

typology.type: Israel as numerous as the sand of the seashore under Solomon
typology.fulfillment: The promise to Abraham fulfilled ultimately in the innumerable multitude gathered to Christ from every nation, Revelation 7:9.

study.christ: The chapter closes by celebrating a wisdom so vast that kings from every nation came simply to listen, verse 34, a foretaste of what Jesus means when He says that one greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42. Solomon's wisdom, though real and God-given, was still borrowed and, as later chapters show, was not enough to guard his own heart from compromise; Christ's wisdom is not borrowed but is His own, and in Him, Paul says, are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, Colossians 2:3. Solomon gave Israel a season of peace on every side, verse 24; Christ inaugurates a peace and a government that will never end, Isaiah 9:7.
study.god: God keeps His ancient promises in visible, countable ways here, a people as numerous as the sand of the seashore, a land secure from Dan to Beersheba, echoing His covenant with Abraham. He is shown to be the true source of wisdom, granting it generously to Solomon exactly as He said He would, and He orders history through the unglamorous, daily details of good government, provision, and peace, proving His care extends to ordinary bread and safety, not only to dramatic deliverance.

crossReferences.note: The LORD's promise that Abraham's offspring would be as numerous as the sand on the seashore, echoed almost word for word in verse 20.
The law's warning that Israel's king must not multiply horses or wives for himself, a caution this chapter's numbers quietly foreshadow being tested.
Jesus declares that something, and someone, greater than Solomon is now present.
In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, the true fulfillment of Solomon's wisdom.

study.oneStory: This chapter is a high point in the long story of promise: God had told Abraham his offspring would be like the sand of the seashore and that his descendants would dwell securely in the land, and here, under a wise king, Judah and Israel do exactly that, eating, drinking, and rejoicing under their own vine and fig tree. Yet Scripture is honest that this golden peace was partial and temporary, resting on one man's wisdom rather than a permanent solution to sin. The whole of Solomon's glittering, God-given prosperity therefore becomes a signpost pointing toward the true and greater Son of David, Jesus Christ, whose wisdom will never be corrupted and whose peace will never end.

quiz.q: What does chapter 4 show as the direct result of the wisdom the LORD gave Solomon at Gibeon?

options.text: An ordered court of officials, a well-run kingdom of twelve provisioning districts, and a land living in security and abundance.
options.note: Verses 1-28 trace exactly this outworking of Solomon's God-given wisdom in the daily life of the kingdom.

options.text: Mainly military conquest and expansion of territory by force.
options.note: Solomon's dominion is wide, verse 21, but the chapter emphasizes administration, provision, and peace rather than conquest.

options.text: A modest, struggling kingdom that only later became prosperous.
options.note: The chapter describes immediate and considerable abundance and security, not struggle.

options.text: Nothing changed in the kingdom's daily life after Solomon's prayer.
options.note: The chapter is filled with concrete evidence of change: officials, provisions, peace, and fame.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Israel being 'as numerous as the sand on the seashore,' verse 20?

options.text: It deliberately echoes God's promise to Abraham that his offspring would be beyond counting, showing that promise now visibly fulfilled.
options.note: The phrase directly recalls Genesis 22:17, marking this as covenant fulfillment.

options.text: It shows that Israel's population had grown very large under Solomon's reign.
options.note: True on the surface, but it misses the deliberate covenantal echo of the Abraham promise that gives the detail its full weight.

options.text: It refers only to the size of Solomon's army.
options.note: The phrase describes the general population of Judah and Israel, not specifically the army.

options.text: It shows that Israel's population was actually shrinking during Solomon's reign.
options.note: The text states the opposite, describing great abundance of people.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of the twelve district governors described in verses 7 to 19?

options.text: Each governed a region of Israel and took a monthly turn supplying food for the king's household, sharing the burden across the whole kingdom.
options.note: Verse 7 states this plainly, and the list of names and districts in verses 8-19 details the arrangement.

options.text: They existed mainly to collect heavy taxes that burdened the common people.
options.note: Provisioning the king's household was a real cost to the land, but the text frames the system as orderly administration, not as a burden emphasized in this chapter.

options.text: They were appointed mainly to reward Solomon's own family through marriage alliances.
options.note: Two governors are noted as married to Solomon's daughters, verses 11 and 15, but this is an incidental detail, not the stated purpose of the office.

options.text: Solomon had no organized system of regional government at all.
options.note: The chapter describes a detailed, twelve-part administrative structure.

quiz.q: Why does verse 29 pile up near-synonyms, wisdom, deep insight, and understanding, to describe God's gift to Solomon?

options.text: To stress that this wisdom exceeded all ordinary human measure, exactly as the earlier prayer at Gibeon had asked and God had promised to grant.
options.note: The layered vocabulary and the seashore comparison both emphasize an extraordinary, God-given gift rather than ordinary cleverness.

options.text: To highlight mainly that Solomon was a skilled composer of proverbs and songs.
options.note: Verse 32 does describe this literary output, but it is a fruit of the wisdom described in verse 29, not the reason for the piled-up vocabulary itself.

options.text: To show that Solomon's wisdom was limited to knowledge of plants and animals.
options.note: Verse 33 mentions this as one example of his wisdom's scope, not its full definition.

options.text: To show that Solomon's wisdom was really no different from that of other ancient kings.
options.note: The text explicitly says his wisdom surpassed all the men of the East and all the wisdom of Egypt, verse 30.

quiz.q: What does it mean that Solomon 'had peace on all sides,' verse 24, and Judah and Israel 'dwelt securely... each man under his own vine and his own fig tree,' verse 25?

options.text: It pictures the fulfillment of God's covenant promise of rest and security in the land, a peace that ultimately anticipates the unending peace of Christ's kingdom.
options.note: This is the settled shalom promised throughout the Torah and historical books, later echoed in prophetic pictures of Messiah's reign, Micah 4:4.

options.text: It simply describes pleasant farming conditions with no larger theological meaning.
options.note: The agricultural picture is real, but Scripture consistently uses it to describe covenantal peace and security, not merely good weather for crops.

options.text: It refers mainly to Solomon's success in foreign military campaigns.
options.note: The emphasis of these verses is rest and security in the land, not campaigns of conquest.

options.text: It shows the land was in a state of war and unrest during this period.
options.note: The text explicitly says the opposite, peace on all sides and dwelling securely.

quiz.q: What might the numbers of horses and stalls in verse 26, alongside daughters of Solomon given in marriage to governors, verses 11 and 15, subtly foreshadow for readers who know the Law?

options.text: They quietly anticipate the very warnings of Deuteronomy 17:16-17 against a king multiplying horses and wives, foreshadowing the compromise told later in chapter 11.
options.note: Kings often lets bare facts carry a moral weight the narrator does not spell out; these details sit uncomfortably close to the Law's warnings.

options.text: They simply show Solomon's immense wealth, with no further significance in the flow of the book.
options.note: The wealth is real and part of the chapter's celebration, but Kings' larger narrative gives these details added, foreshadowing weight.

options.text: They prove that Solomon had already broken God's law within this very chapter.
options.note: The chapter does not yet render that verdict; it simply records details that later chapters will show becoming a snare.

options.text: They show that Solomon had fewer resources than the surrounding kingdoms.
options.note: The chapter describes Solomon as wealthier and more resourced than his contemporaries, not less.

questions: Solomon's wisdom flowed from asking God for a discerning heart rather than riches or long life. What are you tempted to ask God for instead of wisdom, and what would it look like to ask Him for wisdom first?

The whole land ate, drank, and rejoiced under Solomon's ordered rule, yet that peace lasted only a season. Where have you tasted a good season recently that reminded you both of God's kindness and of how fleeting earthly peace can be?

Twelve governors quietly and faithfully supplied the king's table month by month, unnamed and unnoticed by history, yet essential to the kingdom's peace. What steady, unseen faithfulness is God asking of you right now?

Kings traveled from every nation just to hear Solomon speak, yet Jesus said one greater than Solomon is here. Where are you tempted to seek wisdom from lesser sources when the wisdom of Christ is freely offered to you?

Solomon's peace 'on every side' pictures the rest God's people were promised in the land. How does the perfect and unending peace of Christ's kingdom change the way you face the pressures and insecurities of this week?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote follows some LXX manuscripts here; the Hebrew reads 'And he was the one governor in the land,' a minor textual variant regarding Judah's administration.
'The Euphrates' renders the Hebrew idiom 'the River,' the standard way of referring to the Euphrates as the far edge of Solomon's influence, also used again in the same verse.

The BSB footnote notes that some LXX manuscripts, supported by 2 Chronicles 9:25, read 4,000 stalls, while the Hebrew text reads 40,000; the lower LXX figure is followed here as more consistent with the parallel count of 12,000 horses.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 4:1-34. Em dashes in verses 24 and 31 were converted to commas; no other dashes appear in this chapter's source text.
